methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 2 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 3 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Joby Aviation
private.rationale: Same sector (Drones & Autonomous), same stage (Public), post-money $10B+

data.narrative:
Archer Aviation is a public-stage company in the Drones & Autonomous sector that has raised $1.5B+ and currently trades at a $5B valuation. The relevant peer universe consists of six private companies in the same sector, including Joby Aviation, Nuro, Elroy Air, Aurora Innovation, and ideaForge. No public comparables exist in this narrow subsector, which constrains traditional multiples-based valuation.

The peer-median method is not applicable here. The Drones & Autonomous sector contains only three same-sector peers, but filtering by stage alignment, adjacent stages, or similar valuation tiers yields fewer than three datapoints in any filtered subset. Applying an all-sector median would introduce material bias and is therefore not reliable. The absence of both same-stage and adjacent-stage public comparables prevents revenue or EBITDA multiple triangulation.

Given the methodological constraints, a definitive valuation range cannot be anchored to quantitative comp analysis. The $5B current market valuation reflects investor pricing in the public markets but lacks corroborating peer evidence within this nascent subsector. Key uncertainties include the absence of profitable comparables, wide variance in development stage and business model among the peer set, and the limited historical track record of near-peer exits. Further analysis would require either DCF methodology grounded in the target's unit economics, or alternative approach using customer pipeline and TAM assumptions.
